upstream `openusage` reverse-engineering effort and avoids bot filtering.

## Credential sources

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Cascade (read-only — the app never writes any of these):

1. `$CODEX_HOME/auth.json` if `CODEX_HOME` is set.
2. `~/.config/codex/auth.json`
3. `~/.codex/auth.json`
4. **macOS Keychain entry** `Codex Auth` (fallback when no auth file
   exists).

The `codex` CLI owns lifecycle for all sources. The locator never calls
`SecItemAdd`, never invokes `codex auth`, never writes any of these
files.

`auth.json` shape (only the fields the connector reads):

```jsonc
{
  "tokens": {
    "access_token": "<jwt>",
    "account_id": "user-...",
    "id_token": "<jwt>"
  },
  "last_refresh": "2026-05-07T00:00:00.000000Z"
}
```

The active account email is derived from JWT claims (`email` or
`https://api.openai.com/profile.email`) on `tokens.id_token` first, then
`tokens.access_token`. The `email` field also surfaces in the API
response itself and overrides the JWT-derived value at fetch time.

## Sanitized fields

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Drives the leakage test in `Tests/Fixtures/codex-full-payload.json`.

| Location | Field path | Redaction style |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Header   | `Authorization` value | full removal (`<redacted>`) |
| Header   | `ChatGPT-Account-Id` value | full removal (account identifier the vendor treats as private) |
| Body     | any `*token*` / `*key*` / `*secret*` / `*password*` / `*credential*` key | full removal |
| Body     | any `*email*` key | full removal |
| Body     | `user_id`, `account_id` | full removal (account identifiers) |
| Message  | `[A-Za-z0-9._+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` matches | replace with `<email>` |

`user_id` and `account_id` look indistinguishable from each other in the
captured payload (same value); both are treated as private.

## Plan variants observed

_verified: 2026-05-07_

### Plus plan (verified by tester)

```jsonc
<!-- captured: 2026-05-07, plan: Plus, login: redacted -->
{
  "user_id": "<redacted>",
  "account_id": "<redacted>",
  "email": "<email>",
  "plan_type": "plus",
  "rate_limit": {
    "allowed": true,
    "limit_reached": false,
    "primary_window": {
      "used_percent": 30,
      "limit_window_seconds": 18000,
      "reset_after_seconds": 2473,
      "reset_at": 1778114717
    },
    "secondary_window": {
      "used_percent": 39,
      "limit_window_seconds": 604800,
      "reset_after_seconds": 1690,
      "reset_at": 1778113934
    }
  },
  "code_review_rate_limit": null,
  "additional_rate_limits": null,
  "credits": {
    "has_credits": false,
    "unlimited": false,
    "overage_limit_reached": false,
    "balance": "0",
    "approx_local_messages": [0, 0],
    "approx_cloud_messages": [0, 0]
  },
  "spend_control": { "reached": false, "individual_limit": null },
  "rate_limit_reached_type": null,
  "promo": null,
  "referral_beacon": null
}
```

### Pro plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Same shape as Plus. Tighter `limit_window_seconds` on `primary_window`
likely; `code_review_rate_limit` may be populated.

### Spark / Team / Enterprise plans (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

`additional_rate_limits` block expected to be populated with per-model
quotas: `[{"limit_name": "<model>", "rate_limit": {"primary_window": ...,
"secondary_window": ...}}]`. The connector emits a metric per
`limit_name × window`.

### Pay-as-you-go credits (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

`credits.has_credits: true` with `balance` as a numeric remaining
allowance. Connector subtracts from a hard-coded pool size
(`creditPoolTotal = 1000`) to emit a `.payAsYouGo("Credits used", ...)`
metric. Header fallback `x-codex-credits-balance` is consumed when the
body block is absent.

## Metric semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Swift metric | Source field | Reset cadence | Unit | Edge cases |
|--------------|--------------|---------------|------|------------|
| `.timeWindow("Session (5h)")` | `rate_limit.primary_window.used_percent` / `reset_at` | rolling, duration from `limit_window_seconds` (fallback 300 minutes) | percent | missing `used_percent` → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly (7d)")` | `rate_limit.secondary_window.*` | rolling, duration from `limit_window_seconds` (fallback 10080 minutes) | percent | same |
| `.timeWindow("Code Review (7d)")` | `code_review_rate_limit.primary_window.*` | weekly | percent | absent block → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("<limit_name> (5h)")` | `additional_rate_limits[i].rate_limit.primary_window.*` | rolling | percent | one per `limit_name` |
| `.timeWindow("<limit_name> Weekly (7d)")` | `additional_rate_limits[i].rate_limit.secondary_window.*` | weekly | percent | one per `limit_name` |
| `.payAsYouGo("Credits used", ..., currency: "credits")` | `credits.balance` (body) → `x-codex-credits-balance` (header) | n/a | credits | `has_credits: false` → metric omitted; balance clamped to ≥ 0 |

Window durations are returned in seconds via `limit_window_seconds`; the
connector divides by 60 and falls back to fixed values when the field is
absent or zero.

## Time semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

- Reset values arrive as **Unix epoch seconds**: `"reset_at": 1778114717`.
- Connector wraps with `Date(timeIntervalSince1970:)` and emits as ISO
  8601 via `ISODate(date:)`.

## Error catalog

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Status | Connector response |
|--------|--------------------|
| 200    | parse, emit metrics, refresh `lastKnownMetrics`; if no known window block, surface `parse_error` |
| 401    | surface `token_expired`; no metric refresh |
| 429    | preserve last-known metrics, mark active, surface `http_429` |
| other  | surface `http_<code>`; do not refresh metrics |

`identity_unresolved` is logged (no entry written) when both the
response email and the cached email are missing — exceptional state where
the active account cannot be attributed.

## Status page

_verified: 2026-05-13_

| URL | Format | Component filter |
|-----|--------|-----------------|
| `https://status.openai.com/proxy/status.openai.com/incidents` | incident.io public-page proxy (JSON) | `affected_components[].component_id` must intersect the user-subscribed Codex component set |
| `https://status.openai.com/` | HTML page embedding the Next.js RSC payload | parsed once per 24h to discover the current Codex child components |

OpenAI moved off statuspage.io to incident.io; the legacy
`status.openai.com/api/v2/incidents/unresolved.json` path now returns
HTTP 404. The page itself fetches the JSON document above (it lists
every incident, resolved + active).

`CodexStatusConnector` retains incidents whose `status` is anything
other than `"resolved"` AND whose `affected_components[].component_id`
intersects the user-subscribed Codex component set. Incidents whose
only affected components live outside the Codex group (Audio, Chat
Completions, Sora, FedRAMP, …) are dropped. Severity is derived from
the worst entry in `affected_components[].status`:

| incident.io component status | `OutageSeverity` |
|------------------------------|------------------|
| `full_outage`, `major_outage` | `critical`       |
| `partial_outage`              | `major`          |
| `degraded_performance`        | `minor`          |
| `under_maintenance`           | `maintenance`    |

Maintenance windows (`type: "maintenance"` at the incident level) are
mapped to `maintenance` regardless of component status. The clickable
href is built from the incident `id` against
`https://status.openai.com/incidents/<id>`.

Schema reference of an incident entry (fields the connector consumes):

```jsonc
{
  "id": "01KRBZTV511HR7SWVFZJ0438D6",
  "name": "Elevated error rates with GPT 5.5",
  "status": "resolved",                 // "investigating" | "identified" | "monitoring" | "resolved" | scheduled / in-progress for maintenance
  "type": "incident",                   // or "maintenance"
  "published_at": "2026-05-11T16:11:00Z",
  "affected_components": [
    { "status": "degraded_performance", "current_status": "operational" }
  ]
}
```

## Known unknowns

- Pro plan response shape on a Pro-only account.
- Enterprise / Team plan shapes — `additional_rate_limits` content
  unverified.
- Live `credits.has_credits: true` payload with non-zero balance.
- `code_review_rate_limit` populated payload (any plan).
- Headers Codex returns on 429 (Retry-After cadence).

## Source references

- `openusage` upstream project — initial reverse engineering of the
  `wham/usage` endpoint (https://github.com/openusage/openusage) (retrieved
  2026-05-07).
- Maintainer's own captures during development of `CodexConnector`.

## Change log

- 2026-05-07 — initial capture: Plus plan response shape, Unix epoch
  reset semantics, `User-Agent: OpenUsage` header. Cascade order
  `$CODEX_HOME` → `~/.config/codex` → `~/.codex` → keychain.

+component hierarchy, so the discovery layer parses the Next.js RSC
+`{component_id, name, group_id}` triples whose `group_id` matches the
+| Group root id | Observed children (subject to change as OpenAI ships products) |
+| `01KMKF9EBTCD8BN9PG8DJZXRSQ` | Codex Web, App, Codex API, CLI, VS Code extension |
+The cache is refreshed lazily on app start when older than 24h, plus
+on-demand via the "Refresh now" button in the Status preferences tab.
+If parsing fails the connector keeps the last successful cache; on a
+fresh install with no successful refresh yet, the Codex outage banner
- Pro plan response shape on a Pro-only account.

content.text:
Feature implémentée et testée. Résumé :

**Implémentation de l'épic `codex-status-component-subscriptions`** :

- **Modèles** (`StatusComponent.swift`) — `StatusPlatform`, `StatusComponentID`, `StatusComponent`, `StatusComponentsCache(Entry)` indexés par `(platform, host, group_root_id)`.
- **Parser RSC** (`IncidentIOComponentsDiscovery.swift`) — extrait les `{component_id, name, group_id}` de la page HTML via un brace-walker + regex par objet, échoue bruyamment si zéro composant matche.
- **Cache fichier** (`StatusComponentsFileManager.swift`) — actor avec flock + écriture atomique, sœur de `UsagesFileManager`.
- **Registry** (`StatusComponentRegistry.swift`) — TTL 24h, `refresh()` (throws) + `refreshIfStale()` (silencieux au démarrage).
- **Préférences** — clé `statusComponentSubscriptions` (`[String: Bool]`, défaut ON pour clé absente).
- **Resolver** (`StatusSubscriptionResolver.swift`) — convertit cache + prefs en `Set<StatusComponentID>` ; renvoie un set vide tant que le cache est vierge pour ne rien laisser passer avant la première découverte réussie.
- **Filtre connector** (`CodexStatusConnector.swift`) — DTO étendu avec `component_id`, drop des incidents sans overlap ; init legacy conservé (resolver `nil` → pass-through).
- **UI** (`StatusSettingsView.swift`) — onglet "Status" avec toggles, timestamp dernier refresh, bouton "Refresh now".
- **Wiring** (`AppDelegate.swift`) — registry instancié, injecté dans le connector, refresh paresseux après le démarrage du poller.
- **Tests** — 28 nouveaux tests (parser happy/tampered/HTML-unescape/dédupe/ordering, cache CRUD, resolver default-ON/override/fresh-install, connector filter avec/sans abonnement) + 588/588 tests verts.
- **Docs** — `docs/vendors/codex.md` rafraîchi (date 2026-05-13, filter description, group root ID, Change log) ; roadmap nettoyée.

I see the structure now—components don't have their own `group_id` field. Instead, they're nested inside group objects through a `components` array, and the parent group holds the group identifier. So I need to adjust my parser to traverse this hierarchy differently.

My approach: find the group object by locating its `id` field matching the group root ID, then walk backwards through the JSON to identify the enclosing object boundaries, and extract the `components` array from within that object. This means parsing the raw JSON structure manually rather than relying on component-level fields.

For each component I find this way, I'll assign the group root ID as its `groupID` since that information isn't exposed at the component level in the payload. I should also grab a real fixture from the page to use in tests.

content.text: Le vrai schéma n'a pas de `group_id` sur les composants — ils sont imbriqués dans un objet `group` qui porte l'id. Refonte du parser :

input.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import re
with open('/tmp/openai-status.html') as f: html = f.read()
unescaped = html.replace('\\"', '"')
# Find the group object whose id = codex root by walking back from the id occurrence
target = '"id":"01KMKF9EBTCD8BN9PG8DJZXRSQ"'
idx = unescaped.find(target)
# walk back to matching {
depth = 0
i = idx
while i >= 0:
    c = unescaped[i]
    if c == '}': depth += 1
    elif c == '{':
        if depth == 0: break
        depth -= 1
    i -= 1
start = i
# walk forward to matching }
depth = 0
j = idx
while j < len(unescaped):
    c = unescaped[j]
    if c == '{': depth += 1
    elif c == '}':
        depth -= 1
        if depth == 0:
            end = j + 1
            break
    j += 1
group_obj = unescaped[start:end]
print("Group object length:", len(group_obj))
# Find each component object inside it: look at "components":[ and walk
m = re.search(r'"components"\s*:\s*\[', group_obj)
print("components array starts at offset:", m.start() if m else None)
print(group_obj[m.end():m.end()+500])
EOF

content.text:
590/590 tests verts. Le bug est corrigé : le vrai schéma RSC d'incident.io imbrique les composants dans le tableau `components` de l'objet groupe (avec un champ `component_id`, pas `id`+`group_id`). Le parser :

1. Localise l'objet groupe contenant `"id":"<groupRootID>"` (par appariement d'accolades).
2. Extrait son tableau `components`.
3. Énumère chaque objet enfant et lit `component_id` + `name`.

Un fichier `Tests/Fixtures/openai-status-page.html` capturé sur la vraie page sert maintenant de test de bout en bout — il valide que les 5 composants Codex (Web, App, Codex API, CLI, VS Code extension) sont bien retrouvés.

# Smart commit

Tu dois committer les changements git en cours dans le repo courant en suivant les guidelines ci-dessous.

## Contexte utilisateur

Intent fourni par l'utilisateur (peut être vide) : ``

## Étapes

### 1. Analyser l'état du repo

Lance en parallèle :
- `git status` (sans `-uall`)
- `git diff --staged` et `git diff` pour voir les changements
- `git log -n 5 --pretty=format:'%h %s%n%b%n---'` pour voir les 4-5 derniers commits

### 2. Détecter les conventions du projet

À partir des 4-5 derniers commits :
- **Conventional commits ?** Si la majorité suit le format `type(scope): message` (feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf, build, ci...) → utilise ce format. Sinon → format libre.
- **Langue ?** Détecte la langue dominante des messages (français, anglais, autre) → utilise la même langue. En cas de doute, français.
- **Style ?** Impératif vs descriptif, majuscule initiale ou non, présence de scope, etc. → imite.

### 3. Détecter un éventuel fixup

Si l'intent utilisateur mentionne un correctif (mots-clés : "fix", "corrige", "répare", "bug", "issue", "problème", "régression", "broken"...) :

1. Identifie les fichiers modifiés (`git diff --name-only HEAD` + fichiers staged).
2. Pour chaque fichier, regarde `git log -n 10 --oneline -- <fichier>` pour voir quels commits récents l'ont touché.
3. Si **un seul commit récent** (dans les ~5 derniers, non encore pushé sur main/master partagé — vérifie avec `git log @{u}..HEAD` si upstream) est clairement à l'origine du code cassé, propose un **`git commit --fixup=<sha>`** au lieu d'un commit classique.
4. Si ambigu ou si le commit date d'avant `@{u}` (déjà pushé en amont partagé), reste sur un commit classique.

Ne fais **pas** de rebase autosquash automatiquement — laisse ce soin à l'utilisateur.

### 4. Rédiger le message

Règles impératives :
- **Le message explique le POURQUOI**, pas le QUOI. Le diff dit déjà ce qui change. Le message dit pourquoi ce changement existe (motivation, contrainte, problème résolu, contexte métier).
- Sujet court (≤72 caractères), en suivant la convention détectée.
- Si le pourquoi ne tient pas dans le sujet, ajoute un corps (ligne vide + explication). Sinon, pas de corps inutile.
- Pas de name-dropping de fichiers/fonctions dans le sujet — le diff suffit.
- Pas de phrases vides genre « Mise à jour du code » ou « Divers fixes ».

Si l'intent utilisateur est vide ou trop vague pour déduire le pourquoi, et que le diff ne le rend pas évident, **demande à l'utilisateur** avant de committer plutôt que d'inventer.

### 5. Staging et commit

- Si rien n'est staged, stage les fichiers pertinents **par nom** (jamais `git add -A` ni `git add .` pour éviter d'embarquer secrets/binaires).
- Ignore les fichiers suspects (`.env`, `credentials*`, clés privées, dumps volumineux). Préviens l'utilisateur si tu en vois.
- Crée le commit via HEREDOC pour préserver le formatage multiligne.
- **Ne jamais** utiliser `--no-verify`, `--amend` (sauf demande explicite), ni `--no-gpg-sign`.
- Si un hook pre-commit échoue : diagnostique la cause, corrige, re-stage, et crée un **nouveau** commit (pas d'amend).

### 6. Vérification finale

Après commit, lance `git status` et `git log -1` pour confirmer. Reporte brièvement : sha court + sujet + (fixup le cas échéant).

## Garde-fous

- Ne push jamais.
- Ne crée pas de commit vide.
- Ne touche pas à `git config`.
- Si plusieurs "unités logiques" distinctes sont mélangées dans le diff, préviens l'utilisateur et propose de splitter plutôt que de tout entasser. Tu peux utiliser `git stash` (avec `-u`/`--keep-index` si pertinent) pour isoler des sous-ensembles — mais cette commande n'est **pas** auto-approuvée, l'utilisateur devra confirmer à chaque usage : ne l'utilise que si c'est vraiment nécessaire.

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user asked me to implement the roadmap epic "codex-status-component-subscriptions" (on branch fix-incidentio-component-filtering). This feature filters OpenAI status incidents to only show those affecting subscribed Codex child components, with a preferences tab to manage subscriptions. The implementation was to include: component discovery from status.openai.com, caching, preferences for per-component subscriptions (default ON), a Settings UI tab, and filtering in CodexStatusConnector.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - StatusPlatform enum (incident.io, extensible for future vendors)
   - StatusComponentID value object (ULID-based subscription key)
   - StatusComponent model (id, name, groupID)
   - StatusComponentsCache and StatusComponentsCacheEntry (indexed by platform+host+group_root_id)
   - RSC (Next.js React Server Component) payload parsing from HTML
   - Lazy 24h refresh cadence on app startup
   - Default-ON subscription model (missing key = subscribed)
   - SubscribedComponentIDsResolver closure pattern for injection into connector
   - Atomic file writes with flock-based locking (matching UsagesFileManager pattern)
   - Swift actor-based concurrency for file I/O

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - StatusComponent.swift (new): Value objects for StatusPlatform, StatusComponentID, StatusComponent, StatusComponentsCache/Entry
   - StatusComponentsFileManager.swift (new): Actor-based file persistence with flock locking, mirrors UsagesFileManager pattern
   - IncidentIOComponentsDiscovery.swift (new, later refactored): RSC parser that extracts components from status.openai.com HTML
     * CRITICAL FIX: Original parser looked for component_id + group_id on individual objects; real schema nests components in group's components array
     * Rewritten to: locate group with matching id, extract its components array, parse {component_id, name} pairs
     * Key methods: extractComponents(fromUnescapedPayload:groupRootID:), enclosingObject(containing:in:), extractArray(named:in:), enumerateTopLevelObjects(in:)
   - StatusComponentRegistry.swift (new): Actor combining parser + cache + provides cachedEntry() and refresh()/refreshIfStale()
   - StatusSubscriptionResolver.swift (new): Bridges cache + AppPreferences to produce Set<StatusComponentID> for connector; returns empty set on fresh install
   - CodexStatusConnector.swift (modified): Extended DTO with component_id field, added SubscribedComponentIDsResolver injection, drops incidents without subscribed component overlap
   - AppPreferences.swift (modified): Added statusComponentSubscriptions: [String: Bool] property with JSON coding
   - AppPreferenceKeys.swift (modified): Added statusComponentSubscriptions key
   - StatusSettingsView.swift (new): SwiftUI view with toggles per component, last refresh timestamp, "Refresh now" button
   - SettingsView.swift (modified): Added Status tab conditionally if registry available
   - AppDelegate.swift (modified): Instantiated StatusComponentRegistry, injected into CodexStatusConnector, wired lazy 24h refresh on startup
   - Test Files (new): IncidentIOComponentsDiscoveryTests, StatusComponentsCacheTests, CodexStatusConnectorFilterTests, StatusSubscriptionResolverTests
   - Fixture: Tests/Fixtures/openai-status-page.html (real HTML from status.openai.com for end-to-end parser testing)
   - Documentation: docs/vendors/codex.md updated with filter description, RSC payload schema, Codex group root ID (01KMKF9EBTCD8BN9PG8DJZXRSQ), Change log entry
   - Roadmap: Removed codex-status-component-subscriptions.md, updated roadmap/index.md to indicate all epics shipped

4. Errors and fixes:
   - Parser schema mismatch: Original parser expected component_id + group_id on individual component objects, matching old test fixtures. Real status.openai.com RSC payload nests components inside a group's components array with only {component_id, name} fields.
     * Root cause: Parser was based on roadmap notes mentioning the field structure without verifying real page; tests used synthetic JSON
     * Fix: Fetched real status.openai.com HTML, analyzed actual schema, completely rewrote parser to locate group by id, extract components array, parse nested {component_id, name} pairs
     * Verification: Updated all parser tests, added real fixture test (parses real HTML and verifies 5 Codex components found), all 590 tests now pass
   - RSC payload structure: Initial parser tried to extract `group_id` from individual components; real payload has `group_id` only on the parent group object
     * Helper methods added: enclosingObject() walks backward from needle to matching {, extractArray() finds "components":[ … ]
   - Test fixture loading: Initially forgot subdirectory: "Fixtures" parameter to Bundle.module.url(), causing fixture test to fail
     * Fixed by adding subdirectory parameter to match where other payloads stored

5. Problem Solving:
   - Parser validation: Instead of trusting synthetic test fixtures, validated against real production HTML. This caught the schema mismatch immediately.
   - Brace-matching algorithm: Implemented careful depth tracking for extractArray() and enumerateTopLevelObjects() to isolate JSON objects without requiring a full JSON parser
   - Subscription model: Default-ON (missing key = subscribed) avoids forcing users to opt-in to newly discovered components
   - Fresh-install behavior: Resolver returns empty set (not nil) when cache empty, causing connector to drop all incidents until first successful discovery — matches roadmap requirement
   - Legacy path: Connector still accepts nil resolver (old tests), which passes through unfiltered

6. All user messages:
   - "implémente l'élément de la roadmap \"fix-incidentio-component-filtering\"" - Request to implement the roadmap epic
   - "Quand j'essaie de refresh les ids, j'ai une erreur: [screenshot showing error message]" - User reports parser failed with "No incident.io components matched REDACTED"
   - "/commit" command - User requested commit of changes

7. Pending Tasks:
   - Commit the changes (user invoked /commit but I did not execute due to instruction constraints)
   - Optional: Update roadmap notes if they contained outdated schema documentation

8. Current Work:
   I completed fixing the parser bug. The flow was:
   1. User reported "Refresh now" button failed with "No incident.io components matched"
   2. I fetched the real status.openai.com HTML and analyzed the actual RSC payload
   3. Discovered the schema is different: `{"groups":[{"components":[{component_id:"...",name:"..."}],"id":"<group_root>","name":"Codex"}]}`
   4. Rewrote IncidentIOComponentsDiscovery.swift with new strategy: locate group object, extract components array, parse nested children
   5. Updated all tests to use the real schema
   6. Added real HTML fixture (Tests/Fixtures/openai-status-page.html) as end-to-end validation
   7. Updated docs/vendors/codex.md to describe correct schema
   8. All 590 tests pass; real page now successfully parses 5 Codex components

9. Optional Next Step:
   Create a git commit with the changes. The user invoked `/commit` at the end of the conversation. The natural next step is to stage the modified and new files and create a commit with an appropriate message following the project's conventions (likely conventional commits in French, based on the roadmap epic name and documentation patterns). Key files to commit:
   - Modified: CodexStatusConnector.swift, AppPreferences.swift, AppPreferenceKeys.swift, SettingsView.swift, AppDelegate.swift, docs/vendors/codex.md, roadmap/index.md
   - Deleted: roadmap/codex-status-component-subscriptions.md
   - New: IncidentIOComponentsDiscovery.swift, StatusComponent.swift, StatusComponentsFileManager.swift, StatusComponentRegistry.swift, StatusSubscriptionResolver.swift, StatusSettingsView.swift, all test files, Tests/Fixtures/openai-status-page.html

   Commit message should focus on the "why" (fixing real-world schema after discovering the parser was based on synthetic test data) rather than the "what" (the diff shows the implementation). The message might emphasize: "Fix incident.io RSC parser schema to match real status.openai.com payload structure" with body explaining the discovery of the nested components array schema.

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /Users/fcamblor/.claude/projects/-Users-fcamblor-conductor-workspaces-mac-ai-trackers-sarajevo/0419c7d3-dc98-4c98-a07d-2ae496ea93ca.jsonl

file.content:
# Codex (ChatGPT)

> **Last verified:** 2026-05-13 by @fcamblor on ChatGPT Plus plan

<!--
  Read `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` first; it defines the shape of
  this document. If this file disagrees with the contract, the contract
  wins.
-->

## Endpoints

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Method | URL | Headers | Timeout | Response Content-Type |
|--------|-----|---------|---------|-----------------------|
| GET    | `https://chatgpt.com/backend-api/wham/usage` | `Authorization: Bearer <oauth>`, `ChatGPT-Account-Id: <account_id>`, `User-Agent: OpenUsage` | 5s | `application/json` |

The `User-Agent: OpenUsage` value reproduces the header used by the
upstream `openusage` reverse-engineering effort and avoids bot filtering.

## Credential sources

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Cascade (read-only — the app never writes any of these):

1. `$CODEX_HOME/auth.json` if `CODEX_HOME` is set.
2. `~/.config/codex/auth.json`
3. `~/.codex/auth.json`
4. **macOS Keychain entry** `Codex Auth` (fallback when no auth file
   exists).

The `codex` CLI owns lifecycle for all sources. The locator never calls
`SecItemAdd`, never invokes `codex auth`, never writes any of these
files.

`auth.json` shape (only the fields the connector reads):

```jsonc
{
  "tokens": {
    "access_token": "<jwt>",
    "account_id": "user-...",
    "id_token": "<jwt>"
  },
  "last_refresh": "2026-05-07T00:00:00.000000Z"
}
```

The active account email is derived from JWT claims (`email` or
`https://api.openai.com/profile.email`) on `tokens.id_token` first, then
`tokens.access_token`. The `email` field also surfaces in the API
response itself and overrides the JWT-derived value at fetch time.

## Sanitized fields

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Drives the leakage test in `Tests/Fixtures/codex-full-payload.json`.

| Location | Field path | Redaction style |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Header   | `Authorization` value | full removal (`<redacted>`) |
| Header   | `ChatGPT-Account-Id` value | full removal (account identifier the vendor treats as private) |
| Body     | any `*token*` / `*key*` / `*secret*` / `*password*` / `*credential*` key | full removal |
| Body     | any `*email*` key | full removal |
| Body     | `user_id`, `account_id` | full removal (account identifiers) |
| Message  | `[A-Za-z0-9._+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` matches | replace with `<email>` |

`user_id` and `account_id` look indistinguishable from each other in the
captured payload (same value); both are treated as private.

## Plan variants observed

_verified: 2026-05-07_

### Plus plan (verified by tester)

```jsonc
<!-- captured: 2026-05-07, plan: Plus, login: redacted -->
{
  "user_id": "<redacted>",
  "account_id": "<redacted>",
  "email": "<email>",
  "plan_type": "plus",
  "rate_limit": {
    "allowed": true,
    "limit_reached": false,
    "primary_window": {
      "used_percent": 30,
      "limit_window_seconds": 18000,
      "reset_after_seconds": 2473,
      "reset_at": 1778114717
    },
    "secondary_window": {
      "used_percent": 39,
      "limit_window_seconds": 604800,
      "reset_after_seconds": 1690,
      "reset_at": 1778113934
    }
  },
  "code_review_rate_limit": null,
  "additional_rate_limits": null,
  "credits": {
    "has_credits": false,
    "unlimited": false,
    "overage_limit_reached": false,
    "balance": "0",
    "approx_local_messages": [0, 0],
    "approx_cloud_messages": [0, 0]
  },
  "spend_control": { "reached": false, "individual_limit": null },
  "rate_limit_reached_type": null,
  "promo": null,
  "referral_beacon": null
}
```

### Pro plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Same shape as Plus. Tighter `limit_window_seconds` on `primary_window`
likely; `code_review_rate_limit` may be populated.

### Spark / Team / Enterprise plans (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

`additional_rate_limits` block expected to be populated with per-model
quotas: `[{"limit_name": "<model>", "rate_limit": {"primary_window": ...,
"secondary_window": ...}}]`. The connector emits a metric per
`limit_name × window`.

### Pay-as-you-go credits (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

`credits.has_credits: true` with `balance` as a numeric remaining
allowance. Connector subtracts from a hard-coded pool size
(`creditPoolTotal = 1000`) to emit a `.payAsYouGo("Credits used", ...)`
metric. Header fallback `x-codex-credits-balance` is consumed when the
body block is absent.

## Metric semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Swift metric | Source field | Reset cadence | Unit | Edge cases |
|--------------|--------------|---------------|------|------------|
| `.timeWindow("Session (5h)")` | `rate_limit.primary_window.used_percent` / `reset_at` | rolling, duration from `limit_window_seconds` (fallback 300 minutes) | percent | missing `used_percent` → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly (7d)")` | `rate_limit.secondary_window.*` | rolling, duration from `limit_window_seconds` (fallback 10080 minutes) | percent | same |
| `.timeWindow("Code Review (7d)")` | `code_review_rate_limit.primary_window.*` | weekly | percent | absent block → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("<limit_name> (5h)")` | `additional_rate_limits[i].rate_limit.primary_window.*` | rolling | percent | one per `limit_name` |
| `.timeWindow("<limit_name> Weekly (7d)")` | `additional_rate_limits[i].rate_limit.secondary_window.*` | weekly | percent | one per `limit_name` |
| `.payAsYouGo("Credits used", ..., currency: "credits")` | `credits.balance` (body) → `x-codex-credits-balance` (header) | n/a | credits | `has_credits: false` → metric omitted; balance clamped to ≥ 0 |

Window durations are returned in seconds via `limit_window_seconds`; the
connector divides by 60 and falls back to fixed values when the field is
absent or zero.

## Time semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

- Reset values arrive as **Unix epoch seconds**: `"reset_at": 1778114717`.
- Connector wraps with `Date(timeIntervalSince1970:)` and emits as ISO
  8601 via `ISODate(date:)`.

## Error catalog

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Status | Connector response |
|--------|--------------------|
| 200    | parse, emit metrics, refresh `lastKnownMetrics`; if no known window block, surface `parse_error` |
| 401    | surface `token_expired`; no metric refresh |
| 429    | preserve last-known metrics, mark active, surface `http_429` |
| other  | surface `http_<code>`; do not refresh metrics |

`identity_unresolved` is logged (no entry written) when both the
response email and the cached email are missing — exceptional state where
the active account cannot be attributed.

## Status page

_verified: 2026-05-13_

| URL | Format | Component filter |
|-----|--------|-----------------|
| `https://status.openai.com/proxy/status.openai.com/incidents` | incident.io public-page proxy (JSON) | `affected_components[].component_id` must intersect the user-subscribed Codex component set |
| `https://status.openai.com/` | HTML page embedding the Next.js RSC payload | parsed once per 24h to discover the current Codex child components |

OpenAI moved off statuspage.io to incident.io; the legacy
`status.openai.com/api/v2/incidents/unresolved.json` path now returns
HTTP 404. The page itself fetches the JSON document above (it lists
every incident, resolved + active).

`CodexStatusConnector` retains incidents whose `status` is anything
other than `"resolved"` AND whose `affected_components[].component_id`
intersects the user-subscribed Codex component set. Incidents whose
only affected components live outside the Codex group (Audio, Chat
Completions, Sora, FedRAMP, …) are dropped. Severity is derived from
the worst entry in `affected_components[].status`:

| incident.io component status | `OutageSeverity` |
|------------------------------|------------------|
| `full_outage`, `major_outage` | `critical`       |
| `partial_outage`              | `major`          |
| `degraded_performance`        | `minor`          |
| `under_maintenance`           | `maintenance`    |

Maintenance windows (`type: "maintenance"` at the incident level) are
mapped to `maintenance` regardless of component status. The clickable
href is built from the incident `id` against
`https://status.openai.com/incidents/<id>`.

Schema reference of an incident entry (fields the connector consumes):

```jsonc
{
  "id": "01KRBZTV511HR7SWVFZJ0438D6",
  "name": "Elevated error rates with GPT 5.5",
  "status": "resolved",                 // "investigating" | "identified" | "monitoring" | "resolved" | scheduled / in-progress for maintenance
  "type": "incident",                   // or "maintenance"
  "published_at": "2026-05-11T16:11:00Z",
  "affected_components": [
    {
      "component_id": "01JVCV8YSWZFRSM1G5CVP253SK", // matched against the user-subscribed set
      "status": "degraded_performance",
      "current_status": "operational"
    }
  ]
}
```

### Codex component group

incident.io exposes no public unauthenticated JSON endpoint for the
component hierarchy, so the discovery layer parses the Next.js RSC
payload embedded in `https://status.openai.com/`. The payload nests
each child under its group object: the discovery layer locates the
group whose `id` matches the Codex root, then enumerates the
`{component_id, name}` pairs inside that group's `components` array:

| Group root id | Observed children (subject to change as OpenAI ships products) |
|---|---|
| `01KMKF9EBTCD8BN9PG8DJZXRSQ` | Codex Web, App, Codex API, CLI, VS Code extension |

The cache is refreshed lazily on app start when older than 24h, plus
on-demand via the "Refresh now" button in the Status preferences tab.
If parsing fails the connector keeps the last successful cache; on a
fresh install with no successful refresh yet, the Codex outage banner
stays silent until the parser succeeds.

## Known unknowns

- Pro plan response shape on a Pro-only account.
- Enterprise / Team plan shapes — `additional_rate_limits` content
  unverified.
- Live `credits.has_credits: true` payload with non-zero balance.
- `code_review_rate_limit` populated payload (any plan).
- Headers Codex returns on 429 (Retry-After cadence).

## Source references

- `openusage` upstream project — initial reverse engineering of the
  `wham/usage` endpoint (https://github.com/openusage/openusage) (retrieved
  2026-05-07).
- Maintainer's own captures during development of `CodexConnector`.

## Change log

- 2026-05-07 — initial capture: Plus plan response shape, Unix epoch
  reset semantics, `User-Agent: OpenUsage` header. Cascade order
  `$CODEX_HOME` → `~/.config/codex` → `~/.codex` → keychain.
- 2026-05-13 — Codex status filter: incidents now require an overlap
  between `affected_components[].component_id` and the user-subscribed
  Codex component set. Discovery of the Codex child components runs
  off the Next.js RSC payload of `https://status.openai.com/` with a
  24h lazy cadence; the Status preferences tab manages subscriptions
  and exposes a manual "Refresh now" button.

# Roadmap

Open epics for the app. This file is the single source of truth for the features still to implement and their inter-dependencies. Completed features are removed from this index and their files deleted.

Status values: `planned` | `in-progress`.

See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the process and file conventions.

## Dependency graph

```mermaid
graph TD
```

## Epics

_None — every planned epic has shipped._

skills.name: commit
skills.content:
# Smart commit

Tu dois committer les changements git en cours dans le repo courant en suivant les guidelines ci-dessous.

## Contexte utilisateur

Intent fourni par l'utilisateur (peut être vide) : ``

## Étapes

### 1. Analyser l'état du repo

Lance en parallèle :
- `git status` (sans `-uall`)
- `git diff --staged` et `git diff` pour voir les changements
- `git log -n 5 --pretty=format:'%h %s%n%b%n---'` pour voir les 4-5 derniers commits

### 2. Détecter les conventions du projet

À partir des 4-5 derniers commits :
- **Conventional commits ?** Si la majorité suit le format `type(scope): message` (feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf, build, ci...) → utilise ce format. Sinon → format libre.
- **Langue ?** Détecte la langue dominante des messages (français, anglais, autre) → utilise la même langue. En cas de doute, français.
- **Style ?** Impératif vs descriptif, majuscule initiale ou non, présence de scope, etc. → imite.

### 3. Détecter un éventuel fixup

Si l'intent utilisateur mentionne un correctif (mots-clés : "fix", "corrige", "répare", "bug", "issue", "problème", "régression", "broken"...) :

1. Identifie les fichiers modifiés (`git diff --name-only HEAD` + fichiers staged).
2. Pour chaque fichier, regarde `git log -n 10 --oneline -- <fichier>` pour voir quels commits récents l'ont touché.
3. Si **un seul commit récent** (dans les ~5 derniers, non encore pushé sur main/master partagé — vérifie avec `git log @{u}..HEAD` si upstream) est clairement à l'origine du code cassé, propose un **`git commit --fixup=<sha>`** au lieu d'un commit classique.
4. Si ambigu ou si le commit date d'avant `@{u}` (déjà pushé en amont partagé), reste sur un commit classique.

Ne fais **pas** de rebase autosquash automatiquement — laisse ce soin à l'utilisateur.

### 4. Rédiger le message

Règles impératives :
- **Le message explique le POURQUOI**, pas le QUOI. Le diff dit déjà ce qui change. Le message dit pourquoi ce changement existe (motivation, contrainte, problème résolu, contexte métier).
- Sujet court (≤72 caractères), en suivant la convention détectée.
- Si le pourquoi ne tient pas dans le sujet, ajoute un corps (ligne vide + explication). Sinon, pas de corps inutile.
- Pas de name-dropping de fichiers/fonctions dans le sujet — le diff suffit.
- Pas de phrases vides genre « Mise à jour du code » ou « Divers fixes ».

Si l'intent utilisateur est vide ou trop vague pour déduire le pourquoi, et que le diff ne le rend pas évident, **demande à l'utilisateur** avant de committer plutôt que d'inventer.

### 5. Staging et commit

- Si rien n'est staged, stage les fichiers pertinents **par nom** (jamais `git add -A` ni `git add .` pour éviter d'embarquer secrets/binaires).
- Ignore les fichiers suspects (`.env`, `credentials*`, clés privées, dumps volumineux). Préviens l'utilisateur si tu en vois.
- Crée le commit via HEREDOC pour préserver le formatage multiligne.
- **Ne jamais** utiliser `--no-verify`, `--amend` (sauf demande explicite), ni `--no-gpg-sign`.
- Si un hook pre-commit échoue : diagnostique la cause, corrige, re-stage, et crée un **nouveau** commit (pas d'amend).

### 6. Vérification finale

Après commit, lance `git status` et `git log -1` pour confirmer. Reporte brièvement : sha court + sujet + (fixup le cas échéant).

## Garde-fous

- Ne push jamais.
- Ne crée pas de commit vide.
- Ne touche pas à `git config`.
- Si plusieurs "unités logiques" distinctes sont mélangées dans le diff, préviens l'utilisateur et propose de splitter plutôt que de tout entasser. Tu peux utiliser `git stash` (avec `-u`/`--keep-index` si pertinent) pour isoler des sous-ensembles — mais cette commande n'est **pas** auto-approuvée, l'utilisateur devra confirmer à chaque usage : ne l'utilise que si c'est vraiment nécessaire.

content.content:
541961b chore(roadmap): add Codex status component subscriptions epic
Formalizes the feature to filter OpenAI status incidents by subscribed
Codex child components, with dynamic discovery from incident.io's page
HTML, 24h refresh cadence, and user preferences for per-component toggles.

Incorporates review feedback: seed list starts empty (validate parsing
first, add in a separate commit), and cache stores both component_id
(subscription key) and name (for UI context).

Entire-Checkpoint: 7286a2c016f9

---
dc83578 fix(ui): always surface outage banners at the top of the popover
Previously banners were rendered inline before the first card of each
affected vendor, so an outage on a vendor sorted late (e.g. Codex after
Claude) would appear below unrelated cards. Hoist every active vendor's
banner above the card list so users see outages first.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: bc8803dbe89a

---
b9ef463 ci: add Swift build + test workflow on macos-15 (#40)
Runs `swift build` and `swift test` on every push and pull request
targeting main. Mirrors the Xcode 26 selection and SwiftLint plugin
strip used by release.yml and assistant-build.yml so the package
builds cleanly on GHA runners.


Entire-Checkpoint: a1efa8ea8541

Co-authored-by: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
---
46c3c02 fix(menubar): stop status-item render feedback loop pegging CPU (#39)
* fix(menubar): break status-item render feedback loop pegging CPU

Setting `button.image = ...` with a non-template NSImage causes AppKit
to recompose the NSStatusItem replicant view, which flips
`button.effectiveAppearance` — re-firing the KVO observer that just
triggered the render. The loop pegged the main thread at ~600
renders/s (sampled at 96% CPU), entirely inside
`CA::Transaction::commit` → `NSStatusItemReplicantShadowView drawRect`
→ `NSCreateStatusItemShadowImage`.

Two redundant dedup keys break the loop:
- `lastRenderedKey` short-circuits `refreshStatusItemImage()` when the
  combination of segments, text, separator, resolved isDark and
  isUnconfigured matches the previous render — so the no-op assignment
  that propagates back through KVO never happens.
- `lastResolvedIsDark` filters appearance KVO fires so we only re-render
  when the dark/aqua resolution actually changes, not on every spurious
  AppKit-internal appearance churn.

Measured impact (idle, popover closed): 96% → 0.0% CPU; appearance KVO
short-circuit ratio at startup observed at 16/17 fires.

Entire-Checkpoint: 910295e4d170

* test(menubar): extract MenuBarRefreshController and cover dedup logic

The two dedup locks that broke the render feedback loop were inlined in
AppDelegate, where the AppKit dependencies (NSStatusItem, NSImage,
KVO on effectiveAppearance) make them awkward to test. Extract the
pure dedup state into `MenuBarRefreshController` in the lib target,
so the cache invariants can be asserted without standing up AppKit.

The controller exposes:
- `refresh(key:)` — invokes the render callback iff `key` differs from
  the last accepted render; the call that propagates back through KVO
  becomes a no-op and the feedback loop terminates.
- `shouldHandleAppearanceChange(isDark:)` — lets the appearance KVO
  observer filter fires that do not move the resolved dark/aqua flag.
- `renderCount` — exposed for the upcoming integration smoke test.

Tests cover the contract that prevents the regression:
- First refresh always renders exactly once.
- 1000 identical refreshes coalesce to one render.
- Each of the five MenuBarRenderKey fields independently invalidates
  the cache (one test per field, so a future field addition that
  forgets to participate in equality fails locally).
- Appearance fires with unchanged isDark are reported as no-op;
  actual flips are authorised then re-filtered.

Entire-Checkpoint: 1d1a147667cd

* test(menubar): add opt-in smoke test for the render feedback loop

The dedup unit tests cover the controller's contract in isolation, but
they cannot reproduce the actual NSStatusItem ↔ effectiveAppearance KVO
feedback that caused the original CPU spike — that requires a real
AppKit process with a graphical session. This commit adds an
integration smoke test that:

- launches the compiled AIUsagesTrackers binary,
- sandboxes its cache via AI_TRACKER_CACHE_DIR (so the user's
  ~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker is untouched),
- reads the menu bar render count flushed every 2 s by the running app
  (new AI_TRACKER_EXPORT_RENDER_COUNT mechanism),
- asserts the count stays below 100 over an 8 s idle window.

Before the fix the same observation window yielded ~4800 renders
(600/s × 8 s). I verified the test discriminates correctly by
temporarily removing both dedup locks: count jumped to 4299, the test
failed as expected.

The test is opt-in (RUN_SMOKE_TESTS=1) because it:
- spawns a real AppKit process (~10 s wall time, intrusive),
- needs a graphical session for NSStatusBar,
- may be inconsistent on headless CI runners where the
  NSStatusItemReplicantShadowView shadowing machinery doesn't fire.

The binary path is taken from AIUT_BINARY_PATH if set, otherwise from
`.build/<triple>/{debug,release}/AIUsagesTrackers` relative to cwd, so
local developers can run it with `swift build && RUN_SMOKE_TESTS=1
swift test --filter StatusItemRenderSmoke`.

Two small support changes in the lib:
- `Loggers.cacheDir` becomes public and honours AI_TRACKER_CACHE_DIR
  so the test can divert all on-disk state to a temp dir.
- `AppPidGuard` is initialised from `Loggers.cacheDir` instead of
  recomputing the path from `$HOME`, so the sandbox applies to the
  pid file too.

Entire-Checkpoint: 77d5c88e0de8
---
447aad8 fix(menubar): use single source of truth for dot color when percent unknown (#38)
When a time-window metric's percent is unknown (resetAt missing/elapsed,
thus tier == nil), the dot color previously fell back to the surrounding
text color. This caused inconsistency between the real menu bar and the
Settings preview when they differed in appearance (e.g. dark menu bar vs
light Settings window).

Solution: add Optional<ConsumptionTier>.dotNSColor as the single source of
truth. Unknown case maps to a fixed mid-gray (not appearance-adaptive),
ensuring consistent visuals across surfaces. Add a contrasted stroke around
the dot (text color) for visibility against menu-bar wallpapers that happen
to match the fill color.

The fill is now data-driven (segment-identity); the stroke is context-driven
(for legibility only).

All tests pass.


Entire-Checkpoint: bf677a6168ea

Co-authored-by: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
---

content.content:
# Markdown authoring rules

## Language: English only

All Markdown files in this repository MUST be written in English, regardless of the conversation language. This includes `CLAUDE.md`, every file under `docs/`, `README.md`, and any new `*.md`.

- When creating a new Markdown file, write it in English from the start even if the user's prompt is in another language.
- When editing an existing Markdown file that contains non-English prose, translate the edited sections to English instead of preserving mixed languages.
- Code blocks, file paths, commands, and identifiers keep their original form — this rule applies to natural-language prose only.

## Write for longevity

Markdown in this repository is long-lived context. Optimize each file so it does not need to be rewritten as the code evolves. Avoid anything that ties documentation to a specific moment in time or to renamable artifacts:

- Do NOT reference specific commit SHAs, PR numbers, or branch names.
- Do NOT describe the project as being in a transient state ("currently a Hello World", "not yet implemented", "WIP"). If a feature does not exist, simply do not document it.
- Do NOT speculate about intent or future direction ("the name suggests…", "will eventually…"). Document what the code actually does today in terms that remain true tomorrow.
- Do NOT list every file, symbol, or dependency when a structural description is enough — exhaustive lists drift out of date quickly.
- Avoid naming specific files, types, or symbols that could be renamed or moved. Prefer describing roles and responsibilities ("the SwiftUI entry point", "the package manifest") over fixed identifiers. When a path is truly stable because a tool mandates it (e.g. `Package.swift` for Swift Package Manager, `.claude/rules/` for Claude Code), it is fine to use it.
- Do NOT duplicate information that already lives authoritatively in code or tool manifests (version numbers, platform minimums, target lists). Point readers at the source of truth instead of mirroring it.

When in doubt, ask: "Will this sentence still be accurate after the next three features ship, or after a plausible rename?" If not, rephrase or remove it.

## Single source of truth across Markdown files

When two Markdown files cover the same topic, exactly one is the authoritative spec; the other references it and adds only what is genuinely its own (workflow steps, scaffolding procedure, agent phases, etc.).

This applies in particular to these pairs:

- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (skill that implements it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/agents/<name>.md` (agent prompt that uses it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/commands/<name>.md` (slash command that triggers it)

Rules:

- Templates, file formats, naming conventions, and directory layouts belong in the spec, NOT in the operational artifact.
- The operational artifact MUST start with a one-line pointer ("Read `docs/<topic>.md` first; it defines X, Y, Z.") and MUST include a conflict-resolution line ("If this artifact disagrees with `docs/<topic>.md`, the spec wins.").
- Before adding concrete content (a template block, a format string, a list of sections) to an operational artifact, check whether that content already exists in a `docs/` spec. If it does, reference it instead of copying it.

## Exception: `roadmap/`

Files under `roadmap/` intentionally describe planned, not-yet-implemented work. The "Write for longevity" rules above (no transient state, no speculation about future direction) do NOT apply there — a roadmap is precisely a record of intent and ordering that will evolve as features ship. The English-only rule still applies. See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the roadmap file conventions.

content.content:
# Claude Code

> **Last verified:** 2026-05-07 by @fcamblor on Claude Code Pro plan

## Endpoints

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Method | URL | Headers | Timeout | Response Content-Type |
|--------|-----|---------|---------|-----------------------|
| GET    | `https://api.anthropic.com/api/oauth/usage` | `Authorization: Bearer <oauth>`, `anthropic-beta: oauth-2025-04-20` | 5s | `application/json` |

The `anthropic-beta` value matches the rollout date documented when the
OAuth-app usage endpoint was made available.

## Credential sources

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Cascade (read-only — the app never writes any of these):

1. **macOS Keychain entry** `Claude Code-credentials` written by the
   `claude` CLI. Value is JSON of the form
   `{"claudeAiOauth":{"accessToken":"sk-ant-oat01-...","refreshToken":"...","expiresAt":...}}`.

The `claude` CLI owns the lifecycle. The locator never calls
`SecItemAdd`, never invokes `claude auth`, never writes the file at
`~/.claude.json`.

The active account email is read from `~/.claude.json` (`oauthAccount.emailAddress`).

## Sanitized fields

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Drives the leakage test in `Tests/Fixtures/claude-full-payload.json`.

| Location | Field path | Redaction style |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Header   | `Authorization` value | full removal (`<redacted>`) |
| Header   | `anthropic-beta` value | preserved (no secret) |
| Body     | any `*token*` / `*key*` / `*secret*` / `*password*` / `*credential*` key | full removal |
| Body     | any `*email*` key | full removal |
| Message  | `[A-Za-z0-9._+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` matches | replace with `<email>` |
| Body     | `request-id` (response header) | preserved (diagnostic, no secret) |

The Claude usage endpoint response itself does not include tokens or
emails — the field-name based default-deny is defensive against future
schema changes.

## Plan variants observed

_verified: 2026-05-07_

### Pro plan (verified by tester)

```jsonc
<!-- captured: 2026-05-07, plan: Pro, login: redacted -->
{
  "five_hour": {
    "utilization": 50.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day": {
    "utilization": 13.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571503+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day_oauth_apps": null,
  "seven_day_opus": null,
  "seven_day_sonnet": {
    "utilization": 2.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571510+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day_cowork": null,
  "seven_day_omelette": {
    "utilization": 0.0,
    "resets_at": null
  },
  "tangelo": null,
  "iguana_necktie": null,
  "omelette_promotional": null,
  "extra_usage": {
    "is_enabled": false,
    "monthly_limit": null,
    "used_credits": null,
    "utilization": null,
    "currency": null
  }
}
```

Unknown top-level keys (`seven_day_omelette`, `tangelo`, `iguana_necktie`,
`omelette_promotional`, `extra_usage`) are ignored by design — the API
evolves with experimental fields and the connector only consumes the
windows it knows about.

### Max plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Same shape as Pro with additional `seven_day_opus` block populated
instead of `null`. To be confirmed by a tester running on Max.

### Free plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Reportedly returns the same shape with all `seven_day_*` blocks `null`
and `five_hour.utilization` capped on a tighter quota.

## Metric semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Swift metric | Source field | Reset cadence | Unit | Edge cases |
|--------------|--------------|---------------|------|------------|
| `.timeWindow("5h sessions (all models)")` | `five_hour.utilization` / `five_hour.resets_at` | rolling 5-hour window (300 minutes) | percent | `resets_at: null` → emit with `resetAt: nil`; missing block → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly (all models)")` | `seven_day.utilization` / `seven_day.resets_at` | weekly (10080 minutes) | percent | same |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Sonnet")` | `seven_day_sonnet.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Opus")` | `seven_day_opus.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |

Window durations are not returned by the API — they are fixed by
Anthropic's rate-limit policy and hard-coded in the connector
(`sessionWindowMinutes = 300`, `weeklyWindowMinutes = 10080`).

## Time semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

- Reset values arrive as ISO 8601 datetimes with sub-second precision:
  `2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00`.
- Connector strips sub-second precision via `normalizeISO8601(_:)` so
  downstream consumers can rely on the standard formatter without
  enabling fractional-seconds parsing.

## Error catalog

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Status | Connector response |
|--------|--------------------|
| 200    | parse, emit metrics, refresh `lastKnownMetrics` |
| 401    | surface `token_expired`; no metric refresh |
| 429    | preserve last-known metrics, mark active, surface `http_429` |
| other  | surface `http_<code>`; do not refresh metrics |

A `parse_error` is surfaced if the response cannot be decoded as JSON or
contains no known window block.

## Status page

_verified: 2026-05-12_

| URL | Format | Component filter |
|-----|--------|-----------------|
| `https://status.claude.com/api/v2/incidents/unresolved.json` | statuspage.io v2 | none — all incidents on this page are Anthropic-specific |

`status.anthropic.com` 302-redirects to `status.claude.com`; the
connector points at the canonical destination so it does not depend on
`URLSession` following redirects.

`ClaudeStatusConnector` fetches unresolved incidents and surfaces any
incident whose `impact` is not `"none"`. Impact maps directly to
`OutageSeverity` (`critical`, `major`, `minor`, `maintenance`); the
incident `shortlink` becomes the clickable href in the UI.

## Known unknowns

- Max plan shape with active `seven_day_opus` quota — assumed identical
  to Pro plus a populated Opus block. Pending tester verification.
- Free plan shape — assumed Pro-shaped with reduced quotas. Pending
  tester verification.
- Enterprise / Team plans — completely unverified. Behavior unknown.
- The `extra_usage` block's `is_enabled: true` payload — never observed
  by the maintainer. Pending tester verification.

## Source references

- Anthropic API documentation, OAuth usage endpoint
  (https://docs.anthropic.com/) (retrieved 2026-05-07).
- Maintainer's own captures during development of `ClaudeCodeConnector`.

## Change log

- 2026-05-07 — initial capture: Pro plan response shape, fixed window
  durations, ISO 8601 with sub-second precision, beta header
  `oauth-2025-04-20`.

# Claude Code

> **Last verified:** 2026-05-07 by @fcamblor on Claude Code Pro plan

<!--
  Read `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` first; it defines the shape of
  this document. If this file disagrees with the contract, the contract
  wins.
-->

## Endpoints

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Method | URL | Headers | Timeout | Response Content-Type |
|--------|-----|---------|---------|-----------------------|
| GET    | `https://api.anthropic.com/api/oauth/usage` | `Authorization: Bearer <oauth>`, `anthropic-beta: oauth-2025-04-20` | 5s | `application/json` |

The `anthropic-beta` value matches the rollout date documented when the
OAuth-app usage endpoint was made available.

## Credential sources

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Cascade (read-only — the app never writes any of these):

1. **macOS Keychain entry** `Claude Code-credentials` written by the
   `claude` CLI. Value is JSON of the form
   `{"claudeAiOauth":{"accessToken":"sk-ant-oat01-...","refreshToken":"...","expiresAt":...}}`.

The `claude` CLI owns the lifecycle. The locator never calls
`SecItemAdd`, never invokes `claude auth`, never writes the file at
`~/.claude.json`.

The active account email is read from `~/.claude.json` (`oauthAccount.emailAddress`).

## Sanitized fields

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Drives the leakage test in `Tests/Fixtures/claude-full-payload.json`.

| Location | Field path | Redaction style |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Header   | `Authorization` value | full removal (`<redacted>`) |
| Header   | `anthropic-beta` value | preserved (no secret) |
| Body     | any `*token*` / `*key*` / `*secret*` / `*password*` / `*credential*` key | full removal |
| Body     | any `*email*` key | full removal |
| Message  | `[A-Za-z0-9._+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` matches | replace with `<email>` |
| Body     | `request-id` (response header) | preserved (diagnostic, no secret) |

The Claude usage endpoint response itself does not include tokens or
emails — the field-name based default-deny is defensive against future
schema changes.

   Le POURQUOI principal est: 
   - Filtrer les incidents Codex selon les composants auxquels l'utilisateur s'est abonné
